games.title: Off with their Heads!
games.opinion: Overall enjoys the game, especially the sense of discovery in early plays and the cleverness of its trick-taking mechanics where winning isn't always the goal. However, finds it best at 3-4 players, the main strategies can feel linear after several plays, and the poker hand scoring element feels luck-based. The color palette of cards can also be restrictive, and the 'Woods' strategy feels 'all or nothing'.

games.title: Wonderland's War
games.opinion: Mentioned as the world 'Off with their Heads' is set in, an area control bag-building game.

games.title: Title Blades: Barbarian Festival
games.opinion: Another trick-taking game from Judgement City Games with a similar high/low/mid strategy, but the reviewer personally prefers 'Off with their Heads!'.

games.title: Tortuga
games.opinion: A newer trick-taking game from Allplay that focuses on timing wins to get loot in a dungeon.

summary: The video provides a detailed review of the trick-taking game "Off with their Heads!", explaining its gameplay, unique scoring mechanisms across different board sections, and how poker hands are formed. The reviewer discusses the strategic depth of not always needing to win tricks, the evolving value of cards, and the satisfying feeling of executing high-scoring strategies. He also notes some downsides like linearity after multiple plays, luck in poker hands, and a potential "all or nothing" nature of some strategies. The video concludes with the reviewer's final score and recommendations for other trick-taking games.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: Off With Their Heads! Review - Where Would You Like Your Head Cut Off?

games.title: Three Witches
games.opinion: I enjoyed this game, especially the teamwork aspect, the tight bidding for 3 or 4 tricks out of 5, the mathing out of card plays, and the bluffing elements. However, replayability becomes limited once the puzzle is figured out, and sometimes the game can run a bit long. I also have a personal preference for playing as the lead witch.

games.title: The Crew
games.opinion: Recommended for those who appreciate trick-taking with a full team.

games.title: Inside Job
games.opinion: Recommended for those who like the element of team but not team, like a trick-taking game with a social deduction aspect.

games.title: Ghost of Christmas
games.opinion: One of my favorite trick-taking games from Allplay, described as a delightfully good time with a lot of sequencing and very satisfying gameplay.

summary: This video reviews the three-player trick-taking game Three Witches from Allplay, highlighting its unique 1-vs-2 team dynamic, strategic card play, and bluffing opportunities. The reviewer rates the game 3.5 out of 5 (7/10) and discusses its strengths and weaknesses, including its replayability over time. Additional trick-taking game recommendations like The Crew, Inside Job, and Ghost of Christmas are also provided.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: 3 Witches Review - Why Can't We All Just Get Along?

games.title: Giving games to charities/libraries/Goodwill
games.opinion: A feel-good option to get rid of games without monetary return. It's best for lighter, more accessible games and requires checking with the institution (e.g., libraries, youth clubs) to ensure they can use them effectively.

games.title: Selling to Noble Knight Games (bulk buyer)
games.opinion: One of the easiest ways to sell many games at once. You'll receive the least amount of money for your games (around a quarter of their value), as the buyer needs to cover shipping and profit margins.

games.title: Bulk listing on local marketplaces (Craigslist, Facebook Marketplace)
games.opinion: A mid-tier option for getting rid of several games with moderate effort. Offers more money than bulk buyers, especially if you set a price that feels like a very good deal (e.g., selling $450 worth of games for $300).

games.title: Selling through local game stores (events/consignment)
games.opinion: Options vary by store; some may buy games directly, others run events where you sell games for store credit, or offer consignment programs. It requires researching your local stores' policies and events.

games.title: Selling at conventions (used game sales)
games.opinion: Conventions with used game sections (like Origins, Gen Con, Pax Unplugged) can be great if you're within driving distance. Attendees are often eager for deals. Pricing competitively is crucial to attract buyers.

games.title: Selling on BoardGameGeek Marketplace
games.opinion: A good platform to list games individually, reaching a large audience. It requires some effort to list and manage, but can be effective for moving games, especially if offering discounts for multiple purchases.

games.title: Group bartering/giving away within friend circles
games.opinion: A highly favored method, especially within trusted friend groups (e.g., WhatsApp, Discord). You can sell games at a great discount, give them away, or set up a cyclical exchange, fostering community and goodwill.

games.title: Selling on Board Game Exchange (Facebook group)
games.opinion: A large, dedicated Facebook group for buying and selling games, effective for reaching interested buyers. Recommends pricing around 15% below market value for quick sales, and bundling games to save on shipping costs.

games.title: Selling on eBay
games.opinion: Offers wide reach and a 'set it and forget it' convenience. However, it incurs significant fees (PayPal + eBay commissions, around 15%) and shipping costs, making individual sales less profitable. Bulk transactions are harder to set up effectively.

games.title: Trading games on BoardGameGeek
games.opinion: The most preferred method. Allows direct trading of unwanted games for desired ones. It offers maximum value without direct monetary transactions (only shipping costs), especially when doing bulk trades of several games.

summary: The video provides 10 tips on how to effectively get rid of board games, building on a previous video that focused on convincing oneself to declutter. This video focuses on practical methods to sell, trade, or gift games, ranking them from least to most preferred. The host covers options like donating to charities, selling to bulk buyers, using local and online marketplaces, selling at conventions, and trading on specialized platforms. He emphasizes balancing monetary return with effort and convenience, with trading on BoardGameGeek being highlighted as the absolute best method.
_vid_title: 10 Ways To Sell & Trade Your Games

games.title: Earth Siege: Arrival
games.opinion: The game involves one player as the alien invader trying to dominate the board with tokens, while the other player as the human resistance tries to outlast three rounds and prevent alien domination. Players use cards for actions, resource management, and unit deployment. The alien player aims for rapid expansion and increasing the threat level, while the human player focuses on defense and developing technology.

summary: This video is a playthrough of the cooperative board game Earth Siege: Arrival. The hosts, Alex and Professor Meg, explain the basic rules and objectives, with Meg playing as the alien invader aiming for planetary domination and Alex as the human resistance trying to defend Earth. They then proceed to play through the game, demonstrating the core mechanics and player interactions.
_vid_title: Earth Siege: Arrival Gameplay - They're Already Here...

games.title: Spectral
games.opinion: A competitive deduction game that he still enjoys but finds it challenging to get his group to play. He prefers solo or cooperative deduction games.

games.title: The Gang
games.opinion: A cooperative poker game that is gateway-friendly and fun. He still considers it a good game but would usually pick 'The Crew' over it, unless playing with non-gamers more familiar with poker.

games.title: Courtesans
games.opinion: A charming, stock-taking-like game with nice components. He loved it when he first got it and played it a lot, but over time it has seen less play and has shrunk to the background of his collection.

games.title: Ironwood
games.opinion: A delightful asymmetric two-player experience with solo modes. It's an excellent game with great card play that he still owns and likes, but finds two-player games in the 45-minute to 1-hour range harder to get to the table.

games.title: Tír na nÓg
games.opinion: An excellent point optimization game with open table drafting and an interesting puzzle involving piece placement and card claiming. While he enjoys this genre, he tends to default to older favorites like Calico and Cascadia, causing this one to fade slightly.

games.title: Galactic Cruise
games.opinion: An intricate and highly rewarding Euro game. He still considers it excellent, but over time, it has started to feel a bit more like 'work' or a 'sequence' to follow rather than a personally engaging engine-building experience. He's questioning if he needs it personally.

games.title: Things in Rings
games.opinion: An excellent deduction puzzle where players try to figure out hidden rules. It was a 5/5 game for him initially and he still loves and plays it regularly. However, the initial intense excitement has lessened, moving from a must-play to a game he's happy to pull out.

games.title: Harmonies
games.opinion: A very good point-scoring game with charming chunky bits. Despite being considered better than Cascadia by some, he still prefers to play Cascadia due to similar vibes, causing Harmonies to be reached for less often.

games.title: Arydia: The Paths We Dare Tread
games.opinion: The gameplay experience is top-notch, one of the best campaign games he's played, but its campaign nature makes it difficult to re-engage with after a break, as it's hard to remember where things left off.

games.title: Slay the Spire: The Board Game
games.opinion: An incredible game that he loves and owns. However, most of his plays are solo, and the excellent digital video game adaptation makes him often default to playing it digitally to avoid the physical setup and teardown.

summary: The video discusses the top 10 board games released in 2024 that have 'faded' for the reviewer over the past year or year and a half. He clarifies that 'fading' doesn't mean the games are bad; many are still excellent, owned, and recommended, but the initial excitement or play frequency has decreased for personal reasons.
_vid_title: Top 10 Games from 2024 That Faded for me

games.title: Heroes of the Shire: Light and Shadow
games.opinion: The game offers multiple ways to play, including solo/co-op scenario mode and a competitive arena mode. Players build and level up heroes, gaining unique spells and abilities. Scenarios can be 'light' or 'shadow,' impacting bosses and actions. Combat is strategic with no luck involved, focusing on cooldown management and ability usage. There are unique characters with distinct abilities and branching pathways for progression. The game also features diverse enemies, mini-bosses, and exploration mechanics. Difficulty can be adjusted through modification cards.

summary: This video provides a comprehensive preview of 'Heroes of the Shire: Light and Shadow,' a board game currently running a Gamefound campaign. The reviewer explains the core gameplay mechanics, including character progression, spellcasting, combat, and exploration in both solo/co-op scenario mode and a competitive arena mode. She details player boards, enemy types, and how turns and encounters unfold on modular hex maps. The video emphasizes the strategic depth and unique hero abilities without relying on dice rolls for combat outcomes, using dice only for tracking cooldowns and health.
classification: new game including how to play
_vid_title: Heroes of the Shire: Light & Shadow Preview - Enjoy Cooling Down!

games.title: 12 Rivers
games.opinion: A fun and charming game with a high toy factor and great table presence. The villager abilities are fun and offer interesting optimization. However, token powers are often underwhelming or imbalanced, and discarding cards for economy rather than their abilities can feel less engaging. The game has low replayability, with the experience becoming less interesting after multiple plays due to a lack of variety in goals and gameplay, despite the initial appeal of its marble-rolling mechanic.

games.title: Potion Explosion
games.opinion: Recommended as another game with a 'toy factor' involving marbles exploding out of a chute, offering combined abilities.

games.title: Fireball Island
games.opinion: Recommended for a 'completely chaotic' experience that also uses marbles with a high toy factor and significant randomness.

games.title: River Valley Glassworks
games.opinion: Recommended as a game with a similar charming weight factor, slightly more strategic depth, and strong component factor, though with less of a toy factor.

summary: The video provides a detailed review of the board game '12 Rivers,' including a breakdown of its gameplay mechanics such as scoring through villagers and marble collection. The reviewer highlights the game's high 'toy factor' and engaging player interaction but notes some drawbacks concerning unbalanced abilities and declining replayability. The video concludes with a score for '12 Rivers' and recommendations for other marble-themed board games.
_vid_title: 12 Rivers Review - Stop, Drop & Score Your Way To The Top

games.title: Aquaria
games.opinion: Everything in the game works together well, but nothing individually shines. The dial mechanics feel like busywork, and the exploration board isn't particularly rewarding. Early game can feel like a lot of effort for small progress, and end game can become overly mathematical. Despite these points, chaining actions and filling the grid can be satisfying. The game features multiple synergistic systems. Overall, the reviewer enjoyed his plays but it didn't 'click' in a magical way.

games.title: Emberheart
games.opinion: Recommended for those looking for another game of collecting a variety of creatures with worker placement puzzles.

games.title: Finsband
games.opinion: Recommended for those looking for a different kind of fish engine and tableau building.

games.title: The Networks
games.opinion: Recommended as another card-building tableau collecting game.

summary: This video is a rapid-fire review of the board game Aquaria. The reviewer, Alex Radcliffe, provides a brief overview of the game's mechanics, including actions powered by pearls, placing fish cards into an aquarium grid, rotating dials for bonuses, and moving on an exploration board. He shares his subjective opinion on the game, noting that while it is well-designed and functional, it lacks a standout element that makes it truly engaging for him. He also offers recommendations for other games with similar themes or mechanics.
_vid_title: Aquaria Rapid Fire Review - Those Fish Want To Stick Together!

games.title: The Glasgow Train Robbery
games.opinion: A two-player cooperative game based on a true story, with top-notch art and production. The reviewer found the beginning of the game to be very slow-paced with little threat, with the real tension and fun moments occurring primarily towards the end. The game feels like a lot of setup for the final conclusion, which is thematically appropriate but not as engaging throughout from a gameplay perspective.

games.title: Burgle Bros
games.opinion: Recommended as a cooperative/solo game for pulling off a heist with a different gameplay style.

games.title: Core of Discovery Duo
games.opinion: Recommended for its hidden communication tension and working with a partner, without the heist theme.

summary: This video is a rapid-fire review of The Glasgow Train Robbery, a two-player cooperative game based on a true historical event. The reviewer provides an overview of the game's objective to execute a five-step heist plan with limited communication between players, utilizing agents and tools on the board while managing a ticking train timer and unexpected contingencies. The reviewer then shares their thoughts on the game's pacing, tension, and production, ultimately giving it a score and recommending other similar cooperative games.
classification: new game including how to play and rating
_vid_title: The Glasgow Train Robbery Review - We Need More Batteries!
